preamble.summary: Psalm 5 is a morning prayer in which the psalmist cries out to God at dawn, contrasts the character of God (who hates wickedness) with the character of the wicked (who speak lies and practice treachery), and asks to be led in God's righteousness. It closes with a petition for the wicked to face the consequences of their rebellion and for the righteous to rejoice under God's protection.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm establishes the morning as the privileged time of prayer — the psalmist arranges his case before God at dawn and watches expectantly. The Hebrew verb arokh ('I will arrange, set in order') in verse 4 is sacrificial language: the same word used for arranging wood on the altar or setting out the showbread. Morning prayer is presented as a kind of sacrifice. The psalm also contains one of the Psalter's most vivid descriptions of the wicked: their throat is an open grave, their tongue flatters, their inward being is destruction. The exterior is smooth; the interior is ruin.
preamble.friction: The superscription mentions ha-nechilot, which may mean 'flutes' or may derive from nachalah ('inheritance'). The meaning is uncertain. The psalm's call for God to 'declare them guilty' and 'let them fall by their own counsels' (v. 11) is the first imprecation in the Psalter — a prayer against enemies that troubles modern readers. These prayers emerge from a worldview in which God's justice must be visible and the moral order must be vindicated publicly. The psalmist places judgment in God's hands rather than taking revenge personally.
preamble.connections: The morning prayer theme connects to Psalm 3:6 (waking) and Psalm 4:9 (sleeping in peace). The 'open grave' throat image in verse 10 is quoted by Paul in Romans 3:13. The petition to be led in God's righteousness (v. 9) anticipates Psalm 23:3 ('He leads me in paths of righteousness'). The protective 'shield' (tsinnah) in verse 13 echoes the magen ('shield') of Psalm 3:4.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician upon Nehiloth, A Psalm of David.
verses.rendering:
For the director of music. For flutes.
A psalm of David.
translator_notes: Ha-nechilot is traditionally rendered 'flutes' based on a connection to chalil ('flute, pipe'). Some scholars derive it from nachalah ('inheritance') and read it as a liturgical designation ('concerning inheritances'). The uncertainty reflects the broader challenge of psalm superscriptions: they preserve performance traditions whose precise meaning was lost before the superscriptions were fully understood.

verses.text_kjv: Give ear to my words, O LORD, consider my meditation.
verses.rendering:
Give ear to my words, O LORD;
pay attention to my groaning.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with two parallel petitions. Ha'azinah ('give ear, listen attentively') from the root azan ('ear') asks God to incline His ear — a vivid anthropomorphism meaning to pay close, focused attention. The parallel binah hagigi ('understand my murmuring, my sighing') uses hagig, a word that suggests inarticulate sound — groaning, moaning, barely formed thoughts. The psalmist brings to God not only formed words (amarai, 'my words') but also the pre-verbal sighs that words cannot capture. This opening acknowledges that prayer is sometimes coherent speech and sometimes raw sound — and God receives both.
translator_notes: The word hagig ('murmuring, sighing, groaning') is related to hagah ('to meditate, to mutter') from Psalm 1:2, but here it emphasizes the emotional, pre-rational dimension of communication with God. Prayer in the Psalter is not limited to articulate petition — it includes groans, cries, and wordless distress (cf. Romans 8:26, 'the Spirit intercedes with groanings too deep for words').

verses.text_hebrew: הַקְשִׁ֤יבָה ׀ לְק֬וֹל שַׁוְעִ֗י מַלְכִּ֥י וֵאלֹהָ֑י כִּֽי־אֵ֝לֶ֗יךָ אֶתְפַּלָּֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: Hearken unto the voice of my cry, my King, and my God: for unto thee will I pray.
verses.rendering:
Attend to the sound of my cry for help,
my King and my God,
for to You I pray.
verses.expanded_rendering: A third petition intensifies: haqshivah ('attend, give careful attention') to qol shav'i ('the sound of my cry for help'). The word shav'a ('cry for help, plea for rescue') carries urgency — this is not casual prayer but desperate appeal. The psalmist addresses God with two titles: malki ('my King') and Elohai ('my God'). Calling God 'my King' is significant — if David is the speaker, the human king acknowledges the divine King above him. The final clause states the exclusive direction of prayer: ki elekha etpallal ('for to You I pray'). The emphasis on 'to You' (elekha, placed first for emphasis) excludes all other objects of petition. There is no other court of appeal.
translator_notes: The title malki ('my King') anticipates the Psalter's extensive royal theology. God is king over Israel, over creation, and over the nations. The psalms that declare YHWH malakh ('the LORD reigns,' Psalms 93, 95-99) develop what is seeded here: the worshiper's personal confession that God rules.

verses.text_hebrew: יְ֭הוָה בֹּ֣קֶר תִּשְׁמַ֣ע קוֹלִ֑י בֹּ֥קֶר אֶֽעֱרׇךְ־לְ֝ךָ֗ וַאֲצַפֶּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: My voice shalt thou hear in the morning, O LORD; in the morning will I direct my prayer unto thee, and will look up.
verses.expanded_rendering: The time is specified: boqer ('morning'). Repeated twice for emphasis, morning is the psalm's liturgical setting. The verb tishma ('You hear') asserts that God does hear — this is not a request but a declaration of confidence. The second clause reveals the psalmist's practice: boqer e'erokh lekha ('in the morning I arrange for You'). The verb arokh ('to arrange, to set in order, to lay out') is technical sacrificial language — it describes arranging wood on the altar (Genesis 22:9), setting out the showbread (Exodus 40:23), or marshaling an argument in court (Job 23:4). The psalmist arranges his prayer like a priest arranges a sacrifice or a litigant arranges evidence. And then: va-atsappeh ('and I watch, I look out expectantly'). The verb tsaphah means to keep watch, to scan the horizon — the posture of a sentinel waiting for dawn or a watchman looking for a signal. Prayer is not cast into the void; it is presented with care and followed by alert expectation.
translator_notes: The sacrificial overtone of arokh ('I arrange, I set in order') suggests the psalmist understands morning prayer as a kind of offering — words arranged before God with the same deliberation as wood arranged on the altar. This connects to Psalm 141:2, where prayer is explicitly equated with incense and the evening sacrifice.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י ׀ לֹ֤א אֵֽל־חָפֵ֘ץ רֶ֢שַׁע ׀ אָ֫תָּ֥ה לֹ֣א יְ֭גֻרְךָ רָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: For thou art not a God that hath pleasure in wickedness: neither shall evil dwell with thee.
verses.rendering:
For You are not a God who delights in wickedness;
evil cannot dwell with You.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm shifts from petition to theology — explaining why the psalmist trusts God to act against the wicked. Lo El chafets resha attah ('You are not a God who takes pleasure in wickedness'). The verb chafets ('to delight in, to desire, to take pleasure in') is the same verb used in Psalm 1:2 for the righteous person's delight in torah. God does not delight in resha ('wickedness'); the righteous delight in torah. Lo yegurkha ra ('evil cannot sojourn with You') uses the verb gur ('to sojourn, to reside as an alien, to dwell temporarily'). Evil cannot even be a temporary guest in God's presence — not merely forbidden from permanent residence but excluded from even brief proximity. God's holiness is not passive; it actively repels what is wicked.
translator_notes: The declaration that evil 'cannot dwell' with God uses the language of hospitality (gur, 'to sojourn as a guest'). In a culture where hospitality was sacred obligation, saying that evil cannot even sojourn with God is a powerful statement of absolute incompatibility. God does not welcome evil even temporarily.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹֽא־יִתְיַצְּב֣וּ הֽ֭וֹלְלִים לְנֶ֣גֶד עֵינֶ֑יךָ שָׂ֝נֵ֗אתָ כׇּל־פֹּ֥עֲלֵי אָֽוֶן׃
verses.text_kjv: The foolish shall not stand in thy sight: thou hatest all workers of iniquity.
verses.rendering:
The arrogant cannot stand before Your eyes;
You hate all who practice evil.
verses.expanded_rendering: The theology continues with increasing intensity. Holelim ('boasters, arrogant ones, those who are foolishly reckless') lo yityats'vu le-neged einekha ('cannot take their stand before Your eyes'). The verb yityats'vu ('stand, present themselves') is the same word used in Psalm 2:2 for the kings who 'take their stand' against the LORD — the verb of defiant opposition. Before God's eyes, that defiance collapses. The second clause is stark: sane'ta kol po'ale aven ('You hate all workers of iniquity'). The verb sane ('to hate') is applied directly to God. The Psalter does not soften this: God hates — not merely wickedness in the abstract but those who practice it as a way of life. Aven ('iniquity, moral worthlessness, trouble') describes corruption that produces harm.
translator_notes: The statement 'You hate all workers of iniquity' troubles readers who emphasize God's universal love. In the Psalter's theology, God's love for righteousness and God's hatred of wickedness are two sides of the same reality. A God who loves justice must oppose injustice. The hatred is directed at persistent, deliberate evildoing — at those who have made wickedness their craft (po'ale, 'workers, practitioners').

verses.text_hebrew: תְּאַבֵּד֮ דֹּבְרֵ֢י כָ֫זָ֥ב אִישׁ־דָּמִ֥ים וּמִרְמָ֗ה יְתָ֘עֵ֥ב ׀ יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou shalt destroy them that speak leasing: the LORD will abhor the bloody and deceitful man.
verses.rendering:
You destroy those who speak falsehood;
the LORD abhors the bloodthirsty and deceitful.
verses.expanded_rendering: Three categories of the wicked are named and their fates declared. Dovere khazav ('speakers of falsehood') — God te'abbed them ('You destroy, You cause to perish'). Ish damim ('a man of blood, a bloodthirsty person') — one whose hands are stained with violence. U-mirmah ('and deceit, treachery') — one who operates by deception. The LORD yeta'ev ('abhors, detests') such people. The verb ta'av describes visceral revulsion — the same word used for abominations that violate the created order. The psalm builds a profile of the wicked that will be applied to the psalmist's enemies in verses 10-11: they are liars, violent, and treacherous. God's response is not indifference but active destruction and moral revulsion.
translator_notes: Ish damim ('man of blood') literally means 'man of bloods' (plural), suggesting multiple acts of violence. The term appears in 2 Samuel 16:7-8, where Shimei calls David an ish damim — a false accusation in context, but the term itself describes someone whose life is characterized by violence.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲנִ֗י בְּרֹ֣ב חַ֭סְדְּךָ אָב֣וֹא בֵיתֶ֑ךָ אֶשְׁתַּחֲוֶ֥ה אֶל־הֵֽיכַל־קׇ֝דְשְׁךָ֗ בְּיִרְאָתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: But as for me, I will come into thy house in the multitude of thy mercy: and in thy fear will I worship toward thy holy temple.
verses.rendering:
But I, through the abundance of Your faithful love,
will enter Your house;
I will bow down toward Your holy temple
in reverence for You.
verses.expanded_rendering: The emphatic va-ani ('but I, as for me') sets the psalmist apart from the wicked just described. The basis for entering God's house is not personal merit but be-rov chasdekha ('through the abundance of Your chesed, Your faithful love'). Chesed — God's loyal, covenant-keeping, steadfast love — is what opens the door. The psalmist avo vetekha ('will enter Your house') — the temple, the place of God's dwelling presence. The posture is eshtachaveh ('I will bow down, I will prostrate myself') — complete physical submission directed el hekhal qodshekha ('toward Your holy temple'). The manner is be-yir'atekha ('in Your fear, in reverence for You'). Access to God is possible not because the psalmist is righteous but because God is faithful. Chesed is the key that opens the sanctuary.
translator_notes: The distinction between the wicked who 'cannot stand before God's eyes' (v. 6) and the psalmist who 'enters God's house' (v. 8) rests entirely on chesed — not on comparative moral achievement. The psalmist does not claim to be sinless; he claims to be a recipient of God's faithful love. This anticipates the Reformation insight that access to God is by grace, not merit.

key_terms.semantic_range: faithful love, loyal kindness, covenant loyalty, steadfast love, mercy, devotion
key_terms.note: chesed is arguably the most important theological term in the Hebrew Bible. It describes God's loyal, committed, enduring love that sustains the covenant relationship. It is not sentiment but commitment — the love that persists when circumstances give every reason to withdraw. In this verse, chesed is what grants access to God's presence.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֗ה נְחֵ֣נִי בְצִדְקָתֶ֑ךָ לְמַ֥עַן שׁ֝וֹרְרָ֗י הַיְשַׁ֥ר לְפָנַ֥י דַּרְכֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Lead me, O LORD, in thy righteousness because of mine enemies; make thy way straight before my face.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist prays for guidance: necheni ('lead me, guide me') — the imperative of nachah, the same verb used of God leading Israel through the wilderness. The standard is be-tsidqatekha ('in Your righteousness') — not the psalmist's righteousness but God's. The motivation is le-ma'an shorerai ('because of my enemies, on account of those who watch for my fall'). The word shorerai (from shur, 'to watch, to observe, to lie in wait') describes hostile surveillance — enemies watching for a moral slip, ready to pounce. The petition hayshar lefanai darrekha ('make straight before me Your way') asks God to flatten the path, to remove moral ambiguity, to make the right direction unmistakable. When enemies are watching for failure, the psalmist needs God's way to be visible and navigable.
translator_notes: The prayer to be led 'in Your righteousness' (be-tsidqatekha) makes God's character the navigation standard, not the psalmist's own moral compass. This is the Psalter's consistent pattern: human beings do not generate their own righteousness but walk in God's. Psalm 23:3 develops this same idea: 'He leads me in paths of righteousness for His name's sake.'

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י אֵ֪ין בְּפִ֡יהוּ נְכוֹנָה֮ קִרְבָּ֢ם הַ֫וּ֥וֹת קֶֽבֶר־פָּת֥וּחַ גְּרוֹנָ֑ם לְ֝שׁוֹנָ֗ם יַחֲלִֽיקוּן׃
verses.text_kjv: For there is no faithfulness in their mouth; their inward part is very wickedness; their throat is an open sepulchre; they flatter with their tongue.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist now profiles the enemies with four devastating lines. Ein be-fihu nekhonah ('there is nothing trustworthy in their mouth') — nekhonah ('reliable, established, firm') is negated completely: nothing they say can be counted on. Qirbam havvot ('their inward part is destruction, ruin') — havvah means 'disaster, calamity, engulfing ruin.' Their interior life is a pit. Qever patuach geronam ('their throat is an open grave') — this image is visceral: an unsealed tomb that exhales the stench of death. Everything that comes from their throat — speech, breath, voice — carries death. Leshonam yachiliqun ('they make their tongue slippery, smooth') — chalaq means 'smooth, slippery, flattering.' Their speech is polished on the surface while carrying death from within. The anatomy of deception moves from mouth to interior to throat to tongue — every organ of communication is corrupted.
translator_notes: Paul quotes this verse in Romans 3:13 as part of his catena of Old Testament texts proving universal human sinfulness. The 'open grave' image is particularly effective: a grave that has been opened releases decay into the air. The enemies' speech similarly releases moral contamination into the social atmosphere.

The contrast between smooth exterior (flattering tongue) and ruined interior (destruction within) is the anatomy of a deceiver. The psalm insists that God sees both — the outer presentation and the inner reality — and judges by the interior.

verses.text_hebrew: הַֽאֲשִׁימֵ֨ם ׀ אֱ‍ֽלֹהִים֮ יִפְּל֢וּ מִֽמֹּעֲצ֫וֹתֵיהֶ֥ם בְּרֹ֣ב פִּ֭שְׁעֵיהֶם הַדִּיחֵ֑מוֹ כִּ֖י מָ֣רוּ בָֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Destroy thou them, O God; let them fall by their own counsels: cast them out in the multitude of their transgressions; for they have rebelled against thee.
verses.rendering:
Declare them guilty, O God;
let them fall by their own schemes.
For the abundance of their rebellions, drive them out,
for they have defied You.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's first imprecation — a prayer against enemies. Ha'ashimem ('declare them guilty, hold them accountable') from asham ('to be guilty, to incur guilt') is legal language: the psalmist asks God to render a guilty verdict. Yippelu mimo'atsotehem ('let them fall by their own counsels, their own schemes') — the prayer is not for arbitrary punishment but for the wicked's own plans to become their undoing. Be-rov pish'ehem ('because of the multitude of their transgressions') — the basis for judgment is their accumulated rebellion. Hadichem ('drive them out, banish them') from nadach ('to drive away, to banish') asks for expulsion from God's community. The final reason: ki maru vakh ('for they have rebelled against You'). The offense is ultimately against God, not merely against the psalmist. Personal enemies are reframed as God's enemies.
translator_notes: Imprecatory prayers (psalms that call for judgment on enemies) disturb modern readers, but they serve a critical theological function: the psalmist places vengeance in God's hands rather than taking personal revenge. The prayer acknowledges that justice is God's responsibility, not the victim's. It is the opposite of vigilante violence — it is the surrender of the desire for revenge to the divine court.

verses.text_hebrew: וְיִשְׂמְח֨וּ כׇל־ח֪וֹסֵ֡י בָ֗ךְ לְעוֹלָ֣ם יְ֭רַנֵּנוּ וְתָסֵ֣ךְ עָלֵ֑ימוֹ וְֽיַעְלְצ֥וּ בְ֝ךָ֗ אֹהֲבֵ֥י שְׁמֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: But let all those that put their trust in thee rejoice: let them ever shout for joy, because thou defendest them: let them also that love thy name be joyful in thee.
verses.rendering:
But let all who take refuge in You rejoice;
let them sing for joy forever.
Spread Your protection over them,
and let those who love Your name exult in You.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm turns from the wicked to the righteous with an explosion of joy. Three groups are named (all describing the same people from different angles): chose vakh ('those who take refuge in You') — the same word from Psalm 2:12; ohave shemekha ('those who love Your name') — people whose deepest affection is directed toward God Himself. Three responses are called for: yism'chu ('let them rejoice'), yeranenu ('let them sing aloud, let them shout for joy'), ya'letsu ('let them exult, leap with delight'). In the middle stands God's action: tasekh alemo ('You spread protection over them, You shelter them'). The verb sakakh means 'to hedge, to fence, to screen' — God builds a protective barrier around His people. The joy is not fragile optimism but the response to real protection by a real God.
translator_notes: The three verbs of joy (samach, ranan, alats) represent the full spectrum of rejoicing: inner gladness, vocal celebration, and physical exuberance. The Psalter's vision of worship is never merely mental — it involves the whole body, the whole voice, the whole community.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־אַתָּה֮ תְּבָרֵ֢ךְ צַ֫דִּ֥יק יְהוָ֑ה כַּ֝צִּנָּ֗ה רָצ֥וֹן תַּעְטְרֶֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For thou, LORD, wilt bless the righteous; with favour wilt thou compass him as with a shield.
verses.rendering:
For You, O LORD, bless the righteous;
You surround them with favor like a shield.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final verse provides the theological summary. Attah tevarekh tsaddiq YHWH ('You bless the righteous, O LORD') — the divine blessing (berakhah) rests on the tsaddiq, the person whose life aligns with God's order. The closing image is military and tender at once: ka-tsinnah ratson ta'terenu ('like a large shield, with favor You crown him, You surround him'). The tsinnah is not a small buckler but a full-body shield — the large rectangular shield that covers a soldier from neck to ankle. Ratson ('favor, good will, acceptance, delight') is the shield material: God's favorable disposition toward the righteous is what surrounds and protects them. The verb atar means both 'to crown' and 'to surround' — the righteous person is encircled by divine favor the way a head is encircled by a crown. Protection and honor are fused in a single image.
translator_notes: The tsinnah ('large shield') is distinct from the magen ('small shield') of Psalm 3:4. The tsinnah covered the entire body (cf. 1 Samuel 17:7, where Goliath's shield-bearer carried a tsinnah before him). Choosing the larger shield emphasizes the comprehensiveness of God's protective favor — it covers everything.

The word ratson ('favor') describes God's disposition toward the righteous — not merely tolerance but active delight. When God's ratson surrounds a person, that person is enveloped in divine good will. This is the Psalter's alternative to the enemies' hostility: whatever opposition surrounds the righteous, God's favor surrounds them more completely.
